volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: The Casket Comedy (Section 1.1.1 - Section 1.1.98)

sections.label: Section 1.1.1
sections.original: Cum ego ántehac te amávi et mi amícam esse crévi ,
sections.translation: Inasmuch as hitherto I have loved you , and have deemed you to be my friend , my dear Gymnasium , and your mother as well ; so have you shown it to me this day , both you and she . If you had been my own sister , how more you two could possibly have held me in esteem

sections.label: Section 1.1.5
sections.original: néscio , nisi , ut meus est animus , fieri non posse arbitror ;
sections.translation: I know not ; but , according as is my way of thinking , I conceive it could not possibly be , such ready assistance , all other things laid aside , have you given me . For this reason do I love you , and for it a vast obligation have you both conferred upon me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.8
sections.original: Pol isto quidem nos pretio facile est frequentare
sections.translation: I ’ faith , at such a price as this , indeed , it ’ s easy for us to give you our attendance and to do you good offices ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.10
sections.original: ita in prándio nos lepide ác nitide
sections.translation: so handsomely and so elegantly have you entertained us at breakfast at your house , as we shall ever remember .

sections.label: Section 1.1.12
sections.original: Lubénti edepol ánimo factum ét fiet á me ,
sections.translation: It has been done with hearty good will by me , and will be done , to get those things which I shall think you are desirous of .

sections.label: Section 1.1.14
sections.original: Quód ille dixit , quí secundo vénto vectus ést tranquilló mari ,
sections.translation: As the man said , who was borne by a prospering breeze on a calm sea :

sections.label: Section 1.1.15
sections.original: véntum gaudeo — écastor ad téd , ita hodie híc acceptaé sumus
sections.translation: I rejoice that we came [*] to you ; in such a delightful manner have we been here this day received ; nor except in the management , was there anything there at your house but what pleased me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.18
sections.original: Quid ita , amabo ?
sections.translation: How so , prithee ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.18b
sections.original: Raro nimium dabat quod biberem , id merum infuscabat .
sections.translation: Too seldom did the servant give me something to drink , and , as it was , it clouded the colour of the wine .

sections.label: Section 1.1.19_20
sections.original: Amábo , hicine ístud decét ?
sections.translation: Pray , is that becoming to be mentioned here ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.19_20b
sections.original: Iusque fásque est :
sections.translation: It ’ s both right and proper ; there ’ s no other person here .

sections.label: Section 1.1.21b
sections.original: Meritó vostro amó vos ,
sections.translation: With reason do I love you both , who esteem and honor me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.22b
sections.original: Decet pol , mea Selenium ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , my dear Silenium , it befits this class to be kindly disposed among themselves , and carefully to keep up friendships ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.25
sections.original: ubi istás videas summó genere natás , summatis mátronas ,
sections.translation: when you see these matrons of elevated rank , born of the noblest families , how they value friendship , and how carefully they keep it united between themselves . If we do that same thing , if we imitate the same example , still as it is , with difficulty do we exist with their extreme dislike . Of their own enjoyments they would have us to be in want ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.29_30
sections.original: nostrá copiá nil volúnt nos potésse
sections.translation: in resources of our own they would have us not to possess any power , and to stand in need of them in all matters , that we may be their humble servants [*] . If you wait upon them , you ’ d rather be giving your room than your company . So very kind are they before the world to our class ; in private , if ever there ’ s the opportunity ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.35
sections.original: aquám frigidám subdolé suffúndunt .
sections.translation: underhandedly they pour cold water [*] upon us . They declare that we are in the habit of having commerce with their husbands ; they say that we are their supplanters ; they attempt to crush us . Because we are the free daughters of slaves [*] , both I and your mother , we became Courtesans ; she brought up yourself , and I this girl ( pointing to GYMNASIUM ) ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.40
sections.original: ex patribus conventiciis . neque ego hanc superbiai
sections.translation: by chance-fathers . Nor yet for the sake of vanity have I driven her to the calling of a Courtesan , but that I mightn ’ t starve .

sections.label: Section 1.1.42
sections.original: At satius fuerat eam viro dare nuptum potius .
sections.translation: But it had been better to give her in marriage to a husband in preference .

sections.label: Section 1.1.42b
sections.original: Heia ,
sections.translation: Heyday , now ! Surely , faith , she ’ s married to a husband every day ; she has both been married to one to-day , she ’ ll be marrying again to-night . I ’ ve never allowed her to go to bed a widow .

sections.label: Section 1.1.45
sections.original: nam si haec non nubat , lugubri fame familia pereat .
sections.translation: For if she weren ’ t to be marrying , the household would perish with doleful famine .

sections.label: Section 1.1.46
sections.original: Necesse est , quo tu me modo voles esse , ita esse , mater .
sections.translation: It behoves me , mother , to be just as you wish I should be .

sections.label: Section 1.1.47
sections.original: Ecastor haud me paenitet , si ut dicis ita futura es .
sections.translation: I ’ troth , I don ’ t regret it , if you will prove such as you say you ’ ll be ; for if , indeed , you shall be such as I intend , you ’ ll never be a Hecale [*] in your old age , and you ’ ll ever keep that same tender age which you now have ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: multisque damno et mihi lucro siné meo saepe eris sumptu .
sections.translation: and you ’ ll prove a loss to many and a profit to myself full oft , without any outlay of my own .

sections.label: Section 1.1.51
sections.original: Di faxint .
sections.translation: May the Gods grant it .

sections.label: Section 1.1.51b
sections.original: Sine opera tua di horunc nil facere possunt .
sections.translation: Without your own energies [*] , the Gods cannot possibly do anything in this .

sections.label: Section 1.1.52
sections.original: Equidem hercle addam operam sedulo ; sed tu aufer istaec verba .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , for my own part , I ’ ll zealously devote my energies to it . But what mean you amid this conversation , apple of my eye , my own Silenium ? ( never did I see you more sad ); prithee , do tell me , why does mirth so shun you ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.55
sections.original: neque munda adaeque es , ut soles ( hoc sis vide , ut petivit
sections.translation: And you are not so neat as you usually are . ( SILENIUM sighs .) Do look at that , please , how deep a sigh she heaved . You are pale too . Tell us both what ’ s the matter with you , and in what you want our aid ; so that we may know . Prithee , don ’ t by your tears be causing me anxiety .

sections.label: Section 1.1.59
sections.original: Méd excrucio , mea Gymnasium : male mihi est , male maceror ;
sections.translation: My dear Gymnasium , I ’ m sadly affected ; I feel ill , I am shockingly distrest ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.60
sections.original: doleo ab animo , doleo ab oculis , doleo ab aegritudine .
sections.translation: I am pained in spirits , I feel pain in my eyes , I am in pain from faintness . What shall I say , but that my own folly drives me to sadness ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.62
sections.original: Indidem unde oritur facito ut facias stultitiam sepelibilem .
sections.translation: Take you care , then , that you have your folly entombed in that very same place from which it takes its rise .

sections.label: Section 1.1.63
sections.original: Quid faciam ?
sections.translation: What shall I do ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.63b
sections.original: In latebras abscondas pectore penitissimo .
sections.translation: Hide it in darkness , in the very deepest recesses of your breast . Take you care and have it so , that you yourself are alone sensible of your own folly , without any other witnesses .

sections.label: Section 1.1.65
sections.original: At mihi cordolium est .
sections.translation: But I ’ ve got the heart-ache .

sections.label: Section 1.1.65b
sections.original: Quid ? id unde est tibi cor ? commemora obsecro ;
sections.translation: Why so ? For what reason have you the heartache , prithee , tell me , a thing that I neither have , nor any other woman whatever , according as the men say ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.67
sections.original: Siquid est quod doleát , dolet ; si aútem non est — tamen hoc hic dolet .
sections.translation: If there ’ s any heart to feel pain , it does feel pain ; but if there isn ’ t , still this pains me here .

sections.label: Section 1.1.68
sections.original: Amat haec mulier .
sections.translation: This woman ’ s in love .

sections.label: Section 1.1.68b
sections.original: Eho an amare occipere amarum est , obsecro ?
sections.translation: Come now , to begin to be in love , is it bitter , prithee ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.69
sections.original: Namque ecastor Amor et melle et felle est fecundissimus ;
sections.translation: Why , troth , love is most fruitful both in honey and in gall ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.70
sections.original: gustui dat dulce , amarum ad satietatem usque oggerit .
sections.translation: inasmuch as it produces sweetness in a mere taste , but causes bitterness even to repletion .

sections.label: Section 1.1.71
sections.original: Ad istam faciem est morbus , qui me , mea Gymnasium , macerat .
sections.translation: Of that character is the malady that afflicts me , my dear Gymnasium .

sections.label: Section 1.1.72
sections.original: Perfidiosus est Amor .
sections.translation: Love is full of treachery .

sections.label: Section 1.1.72b
sections.original: Ergo in mé peculatum facit .
sections.translation: He ’ s taking his spoils of me , then .

sections.label: Section 1.1.73
sections.original: Bono animo es , erit isti morbo melius .
sections.translation: Be of good courage , you ’ ll get the better of this malady .

sections.label: Section 1.1.73b
sections.original: Confidam fore ,
sections.translation: I trust it will be so , if the physician comes that can administer the medicine to this malady .

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.original: Veniet .
sections.translation: He will come .

sections.label: Section 1.1.75b
sections.original: Spissum istuc amanti est verbum , veniet , nisi venit .
sections.translation: A hard expression is that to one in love , He will come , unless he does come . But by my own fault and foolishness , am I , wretched creature , more afflicted , because for him alone have I longed for myself , with whom to pass my life .

sections.label: Section 1.1.78
sections.original: Matronae magis cónducibilest istuc , mea Selenium ,
sections.translation: That is more suitable to a married woman , my dear Silenium , to love but one , and with him to pass her life , to whom she has once been married ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.80
sections.original: verum enim méretrix fortunati est oppidi simillima :
sections.translation: but , indeed , a Courtesan is most like a flourishing city ; she cannot alone increase her fortunes without a multitude of men .

sections.label: Section 1.1.82
sections.original: Hoc volo agatis . qua accersitae causa ad me estis , eloquar .
sections.translation: I want you to give heed to this matter ; the thing on account of which you have been sent for to me , I ’ ll disclose . Now , my mother , because I don ’ t wish myself to be called a Courtesan , complied with my desire ; in that matter she indulged myself who have been obedient to her ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.original: ut me , quém ego amarem graviter , sineret cúm eo vivere .
sections.translation: to allow me to live with him alone whom I so ardently loved .

sections.label: Section 1.1.86
sections.original: Stulte ecastor fecit . sed tu enumquam cum quiquam viro
sections.translation: I ’ faith , she acted foolishly . But look , have you ever kept company with any man ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.87b
sections.original: Nisi quidem cum Alcesimarcho , nemine ,
sections.translation: With no one , indeed , except Alcesimarchus ; nor has any other person whatever committed an infringement on my chastity .

sections.label: Section 1.1.88b
sections.original: Obsecro ,
sections.translation: Prithee , by what means did this man gain your good graces ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.89b
sections.original: Per Dionysia
sections.translation: At the festival of Bacchus

sections.label: Section 1.1.90
sections.original: mater pompam me spectatum duxit . dum redeo domum ,
sections.translation: my mother took me to see the procession . While I was returning home , from a secret look-out he secretly traced me even to the door ; after that , he insinuated himself into the friendship of my mother and myself as well , by endearments , presents , and gifts .

sections.label: Section 1.1.93b
sections.original: Mihi istunc vellem hominem dari ,
sections.translation: I should like a man of that sort to be offered me . How I ’ d work him .

sections.label: Section 1.1.94b
sections.original: Quid opust verbis ? consuetudine
sections.translation: What need is there of words ? Through intercourse , I on the other hand began to love him , and he myself .

sections.label: Section 1.1.95b
sections.original: O mea Selenium ,
sections.translation: O my dear Silenium —!

sections.label: Section 1.1.96
sections.original: adsimulare amare oportet . nam si ames , extempulo
sections.translation: You ought to pretend to be in love ; for if you fall in love at once , you ’ ll be much better consulting the interests of him whom you love than your own .

sections.label: Section 1.1.98
sections.original: At ille conceptis iuravit verbis apud matrem meam ,
sections.translation: But in solemn form he took an oath before my mother that he would take me as his wife . Now , another woman is about to be taken home by him ,
